topics.title: Rural Farming and Meat Consumption
topics.summary: The studio explores the complexities of rural life, discussing the history of hunting squirrels and raising livestock for food versus the emotional toll of consuming animals that are perceived as highly evolved.

topics.title: Farm Life, Vampire Movies, and Halloween Traditions
topics.summary: The studio explores personal anecdotes about farm life, the decline of record labels, and the cultural impact of Halloween while discussing upcoming performances and local events.

topics.title: Hygiene Rituals and Living Conditions
topics.summary: The studio features a conversation regarding personal hygiene routines involving baby wipes and napkins, as well as the life of a man living in a rooming house with a bucket for waste.

topics.title: New York Yankees Management and Baseball Analysis
topics.summary: The studio discusses the transition from Joe Torre to Joe Girardi as manager of the New York Yankees, debating the impact on team chemistry and the potential for a new era in baseball.

topics.title: Artie Lange's Diet and Gift Acknowledgments
topics.summary: The studio discusses Artie Lange's adherence to his diet, a list of gifts he received from colleagues, and the upcoming holiday party plans.

topics.title: Beetlejuice and Bob Levy Confrontation
topics.summary: The studio features a heated exchange between Beetlejuice and Bob Levy regarding personal hygiene, past incidents involving a fan named Sal, and stories of backstage encounters.

topics.title: Radio Industry Shifts and Guest Appearances
topics.summary: The studio discusses the changing landscape of radio formats in Chicago, the unique success of Howard Stern's show compared to other programs, and features a segment with comedian Beadlejuice before preparing for an upcoming appearance by Duran Duran.

topics.title: Tributes to Robert Goulet and Johnny Cash
topics.summary: The show pays tribute to the recent passing of entertainers Robert Goulet and Johnny Cash while discussing their careers and personal lives.

topics.title: Duran Duran Interview
topics.summary: The studio hosts a discussion with members of Duran Duran regarding their career history, personal lives, past rivalries with other artists, and the evolution of their public image.

topics.title: Duran Duran Album and Tour Promotion
topics.summary: The studio hosts Duran Duran to discuss their new album "Red Carpet Massacre," the single "Falling Down," and an upcoming Broadway tour.

topics.title: Seinfeld Cookbook Controversy and Media Updates
topics.summary: The studio discusses the public dispute between Jerry Seinfeld's wife and another author over similar cookbook concepts, followed by a news segment covering Benji Bronk’s premiere and Artie Lange’s media appearances.

topics.title: Staff Tensions and Workplace Dynamics
topics.summary: The studio explores internal conflicts between staff members, specifically addressing the perceived favoritism toward Artie and a heated confrontation involving Sal's aggressive behavior.

topics.title: Internal Show Dynamics and Personnel Conflict
topics.summary: The studio explores internal tensions regarding professional boundaries, the perceived maturity of staff members, and the specific interpersonal dynamics between Sal, Richard, and Artie.

topics.title: Celebrity News and Current Events
topics.summary: The show covers a variety of topics including celebrity rumors involving Lance Armstrong and the Olsen twins, Bill Maher's public persona, Ellen DeGeneres' ratings, and various news reports regarding local recalls, legal cases, and sports management.

topics.title: Political Debate Analysis and Celebrity News
topics.summary: The studio discusses a presidential debate involving Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama before transitioning to celebrity news regarding David Copperfield, Robert Goulet, and various Halloween-related anecdotes.

transcript.text: Yeah, good morning everybody.
I have to clear my throat.
Bobabooie was in some weird clearing throat thing today
when he was explaining to me what was on our agenda.
He, I mean, he was... [clears throat]
Uh-oh.
That's a lot of phlegm.
Oh, I was gonna throw up.
It's that time of year.
It's phlegm time.
Morph, I'm having a hard day.
He announced it?
No.
Because even I'm, like, a little dry...
Oh, no.
You got a cold.
No, it's just the...
I didn't have any moisture and...
Too many berries.
Too many berries.
You need to eat something.
Too many berries.
You need to eat a bird or...
Have a sandwich.
Yeah, if I eat a piece of chicken, does it go away?
You need to eat a fish or something.
You need something living.
You need a roast?
Roast be some provolone.
You know, people are now eating squirrels.
Really?
Yeah.
And I'm sure Richard's dead.
Oh, I'm sure.
He's eaten lots of things.
Roadkill.
He told us he had squirrels.
Yeah, you're...
I mean, like, when we first met Richard, I think you said his father eats squirrels.
Is that right?
Yeah, I think it's been, like, a common thing for a while.
You...
Yeah, well, what happened was, there was a warning put out that you shouldn't eat squirrels
because...
Fuck my warning.
We don't eat them because they're rats.
I mean, yeah.
With fluffy tails.
They look filthy.
But they are.
But the warning was that they were very toxic, that they were filled with lead.
Yeah.
And then they...
Where did they pick up lead?
Well, that's the funny part of the story.
I guess some researchers were seeing if it was safe to eat squirrel.
So they put their guts, basically, in a blender and blend them up.
And they looked around and they saw that there was high levels of lead.
What they failed to realize at first was that the blender had lead in it.
And so the squirrels are fine to eat.
I just want you to know that.
Did you guys eat squirrel?
Oh, yeah.
All the time.
Yeah.
Yeah.
The guy in the paper said, "It doesn't taste like chicken.
It tastes like squirrel."
It is.
I didn't like it.
I ate it when I was a kid and I didn't...
It reminds me of rabbit, if you've ever had rabbit.
No.
It's kind of similar, like, real dark.
Well, isn't rabbit supposed to be, like, a French delicacy, though?
Is that true?
Yeah, I love it.
Howard, I don't know.
Sure.
The one guy said that he enjoys the leg of the squirrel, the hind leg.
Ooh.
That's the best part?
Yeah.
Well, that would be the most muscular.
Your dad, like, he knows no fear.
He'll eat anything that lives.
Well, the town that he grew up in, he introduced squirrels to that area.
He made squirrel raviolis.
Well, he...
What does he mean he introduced squirrels?
He caught a squirrel, I guess.
You've never heard of squirrel before?
No, there were no squirrels in this town he grew up in.
He was a squirrel importer.
Yeah, really.
He got squirrels from, I don't know, out of Missouri or something.
Johnny Squirrelseed.
And he brought squirrels into this town and they started reproducing and in a few years,
he said they were everywhere and that's when they started hunting them.
So, your father's the direct opposite of St. Patrick.
He marched the rats into a town.
Yeah, yeah.
Wow.
I don't think there are going to be any statues erected to him.
I can't imagine.
I cannot imagine eating a squirrel.
Look, if I had a...
Right.
You know, I'd eat anything, but...
But in New York City and stuff, they're kind of like you see them in Central Park
and they're real cute and stuff, but out in the wild,
you just see them running around in trees.
They're more kind of like wild animals out there.
How many of you have to kill them put together a decent meal?
I would think you'd have to eat like six or seven of them.
Well, I mean, if you're...
70 makes a nice squirrel salad.
How many do you have to kill to eat like for two?
Probably two or three.
Let's say one squirrel per person.
You can get that much meat off of a squirrel?
Yeah.
Why?
They're a little meatier than they look.
No kidding.
I would be more ashamed of that than cannibalism.
I'd rather eat a person.
You know, I just saw a documentary about the Donner Party.
Right.
And for years, their family wanted to prove that, no,
they didn't eat the other people.
And supposedly, one of the women who was a descendant
of one of the ones who survived said,
"Oh, no, now there's scientists that have proven
that we didn't eat other people.
My pair, my great-grandfather, didn't eat another person."
But to me, I'd rather eat another person than a squirrel.
Isn't it really hard to kill a squirrel?
Like, I mean, to actually take your rifle and hit it?
Have you ever shot one?
I haven't, but my dad does.
You said your father's a great shot.
Yeah.
And your dad can hit it from how far away?
Well, they're probably about 20 to 25 feet up in a tree.
Headshot?
Yeah.
Wow.
If you hit him in the body, it's almost like all the meat's gone.
But, yeah, it'll kind of ruin the meat.
But, yeah, it'll still knock him out of the tree
if you hit him in the body.
But you want to try and get the head.
What are you using?
Probably a .308 or something like that.
Yeah.
Like a rifle or a .22.
And your dad can hit a squirrel on the run?
Mm-hmm.
Wow.
My dad has amazing eyesight.
Like, we'll be driving down the road,
and he can see a squirrel out of the corner of his eye in a tree
that's like half a mile away.
And will he run out of the car and kill it?
Just so he'll have dinner that night?
No, not usually.
He'll just say, "Hey, look over there."
You see that squirrel, or he'll see a deer from a mile away
without even just driving down the road off the side.
He's a real hunk of it.
Yeah.
That's his thing.
Like, he notices animals all around him without even looking for him.
Well, who do you think would be better at survival?
Chuck Zito or his dad?
Mm-hmm.
Who you want, like, if you're stranded in the woods?
Yeah.
Yeah, I think you want Richard's dad,
but in an urban setting, you want Chuck Zito.
I think Chuck--
There are different kinds of animals.
Yeah, because Chuck will organize people and get them doing everything for him.
I think Chuck will have better stories, too.
Oh, I'm a Richard's father.
He's got some great stories, I bet, right?
He's got some wild ones.
Richard, grab the wheel.
I'm going to get us dinner.
Did your dad ever pull over and go out and get dinner and then just shoot some stuff?
Not really, because he's more of a sportsman.
If he sees it on the side of the road and goes to get it, that's not really a sport.
He'd rather go out in the woods and spend a day going after the animal.
That's more sportsman-like.
Oh, wow. That's cool.
Does he have certain rules of fishing, too?
Oh, yeah, real rules.
No, not really.
Fish aren't as hard to get.
Me and a bunch of my buddies at the Jersey Shore when I was in high school,
we had this robo we took out by the Barnagut Bridge.
It was nighttime, and we would shine a flashlight on the water,
and those crabs would swim up to the light, and we'd net them.
We got busted.
The Coast Guard came and said, "You can be arrested for that
because it's not sportsman-like."
Yeah.
There are rules, like, you've got to get fair.
It's not fair to the crab.
There's something called noodling that's only legal in Oklahoma.
You told us about that.
Yeah, where you go and grab the catfish out of their holes in the water
when they're spawning.
They're, like, laying their eggs in these holes in a certain time of year,
and people can go reach into these holes, feel around.
They'll feel a catfish and grab its mouth and pull it out.
Sometimes the catfish are, like, 80 pounds,
and there's people in Kansas you'll see with their fingers missing,
or in Oklahoma with their fingers missing,
because they reached in and grabbed a turtle.
Yeah.
Wow.
Stop a turtle.
Imagine losing your finger to a turtle.
That's what all those missing fingers are about.
Yeah.
I was wondering.
I knew what the missing teeth were from.
I didn't know what the missing fingers were from.
Usually noodling, yeah.
I thought they were all shop teachers.
Yeah.
Your old man's something else, man.
Yeah, no.
I mean, that's the guy you want.
You know, like, your father was in Vietnam, too.
That's the kind of guy who goes.
Like, they get these tough motherfuckers out there.
And then when they get back from Vietnam,
they all move to, like, Kansas and get in the rural area.
Because they'll hunt and--
They'll still kill each other.
Well, when I was growing up, all I ate was catfish,
because he fished all the time.
Quail.
We ate a lot of quail, because he loved to quail hunt.
Pigeons.
Raccoons.
We never ate pigeons.
They were too much-- I mean, pigeons, I think,
carry a lot of disease.
Mm-hmm.
So we never ate pigeons.
But just an all-wild game.
That's what we lived on.
Raccoons is the one that I cannot believe.
Yeah.
That just looks like nasty shit.
Yeah, you might as well be eating a ton of squirrels.
That's horrible.
Squirrels are a little nastier than raccoons.
Should dad and mom never go to the supermarket
and just get, like, a steak or something?
Oh, yeah, they do that, too.
Yeah.
Like, my dad makes an awesome hamburger, too.
But just-- it's so much cheaper.
And they love the sport of hunting and fishing.
What do you mean?
What does your mom do in all this?
Oh, you never watched the special in his family.
No.
She's got a huge part in this.
I mean, the dad goes out and kills it.
He takes it back to this house of horrors.
I mean, the barn.
And that's where he skins them.
And then you can watch that whole--
there's a special on Howard TV.
I don't know if it's still up there,
because we rotate shows a lot.
But there was one with Richard's parents.
And then they-- the dad skins them.
And once he skins them, then it's over to mom.
Mom prepares the whole thing, cooks the whole thing.
I mean, she spends all day on it.
Wow.
Because, you know, you've got to get that squirrel
and raccoon taste out of that meat.
I mean, you just can't--
you just can't fry that up and eat it.
Oh, is that right?
Yeah, she stews it for the whole day.
There's onions.
There's all kinds of crap.
Beer.
Sometimes you put it in beer.
Yeah, you spend the day trying to get rid of the fact
that you're eating a raccoon.
Right.
You know what I mean?
Yeah, you got to get rid of what it is to get it down.
It's vile.
But when you think about it, like, how--
You got to do it.
How is a chicken that much different than a raccoon?
You want to know something?
It really isn't.
It's a bird.
You're eating a bird.
Yeah.
Every time I say that, I'm going to throw up.
Yeah, you guys are eating birds now.
Yeah.
Like chickens, actually.
I don't do it anymore.
Chickens actually probably eat as much junk
and gross stuff as raccoons.
Oh, yeah.
It's all-- you know, it's all when you think about what we're doing,
like something with a face and eyes and meat.
Brr, brr.
And it's hard sometimes growing up on a farm
because we have pet chickens.
Like, we had one named Shithead,
and we had chickens that are tamer that we won't eat,
but it's still hard when you get attached to a chicken
and then you're eating chicken that much.
And I'm sure you've eaten a lot of Shithead's friends.
Yeah, well--
That's weird, making one a pet and then eating the other ones.
Well, when I was a kid, we had a calf that was my pet for about a year.
And then what?
Well, then he had to go bye-bye and go to the--
Oh, no.
Did you beg your dad not to eat him?
I didn't beg, but I cried, like when we put him in the trailer to take him away.
And what'd you-- like, that's pathetic and heartbreaking.
Right.
Did your dad, like, respond at all?
Yeah, yeah, but did it make you eat it?
I did eat it, yeah.
You said you had to eat it?
No, he didn't say I had to, but I just did.
When you're on a farm, that's kind of part of life.
You know, I used to feed all the animals,
so I knew I was attached to all the animals.
Does it make you sad to think about that?
It does, yeah.
You need all your little friends.
Yeah.
Makes me sad that, you know, a calf is pretty evolved.
You know, they can even follow commands and they can--
they think kind of like-- like my dog.
We'll say to my dog, my dog has a bed on the other side of the apartment
because she snores.
And I go, Bianca, go to bed now and I'll get you in the morning.
And she gets up and she walks over to her bed.
I mean, she's pretty evolved.
And now you're going to eat her?
That's what I mean.
Like, doesn't the calf, like, even respond to somebody?
Like, when the calf would see you, would it get excited?
It would come over to me and I could pet it
and I would go out in the field and see it.
But when we bought the calf, we bought it specifically for eating.
It wasn't bought-- if it was bought as my bed--
How old were you?
You were a little kid.
I was probably 10 or 11.
Yeah, you don't understand that.
Yeah.
You see this wonderful little creature and--
Could you imagine eating Bianca?
Yeah, that's what I mean.
I mean, one day we'd go, well, we're hungry.
But I wasn't as close to this calf as you are to Bianca.
Right.
But it was something that I would go feed every day and pet.
And, you know, usually you're not petting your food before you eat it.
Yeah, man.
That's tough.
Yeah.
When you talk about what chickens eat, that special I saw on killer ants,
they got into this village and they got into the chicken coop.
These ants and the chicken-- you know, the chicken were eating all the killer ants.
They'll eat anything.
Yeah.
I mean, there's cabbage.
Did the calf have a name?
I think like Cathy or something.
The naming of the animals out there is really crazy.
You're as bad at naming animals as I was.
I had little pets.
I told you my frog was dainty jainty.
See, but that's a little creative.
But Caffee is really bad.
Dainty jainty isn't much better, like Caffee.
We were just lazy when it came to names.
I'm not a name.
What is this again?
A cow?
I want to name him Cowie.
And we have me and my sister--
This is Froggy.
Me and my sister were--
Yeah, you just put an I.E. on everybody.
This is Chicken.
Caddy.
Caddy.
Me and my sister were in 4-H, so we'd get hogs every summer to raise for the county fair.
And hogs are the same as cows.
Like if you raise them, they get kind of tame.
Well, they say pigs are--
Are very intelligent.
Are smarter than dogs.
Yeah.
They're great pets, but these hogs would get huge.
I mean, several hundred pounds.
So we couldn't really keep them as pets, but we'd take them to the county fair
and then show them like at a show where we went ribbons.
But then it was so sad because at the end of the county fair,
all the big trucks come in to take the pigs away.
And you see just all these kids that are so sad that their hogs
are going off to the slaughterhouse.
It was like Schindler's Pig.
It was. It was-- It's really sad.
And did you feel like, gee, maybe I could save one of the pigs or--
Well, I mean--
I couldn't do it.
Yeah, I mean, you could probably keep them,
but the whole point of doing it was they get a certain age
and it's just time for them to be food.
And they're poor people. They need the money or the food.
Well, that was it, too.
That was a big part of our income.
Me and my sister would sell these pigs at the county fair.
They'd bring like $250 apiece.
And so me and my sister each get $500. That's a--
That's a lot of money.
A lot of money.
Plus, feeding a pig is really expensive.
I mean, you can feed them scraps and stuff, too,
but mostly we'd buy these big feed bags that were really expensive.
So you got to make a return on your investment.
Yeah.
They can taste too good to save those pigs, man.
Yeah.
Oh, boy. That's tough, man, eating your pet.
Seems like a lot of hard work just to, you know, not the animal.
Well, that's a different lifestyle out there, definitely.
When you ate cafe, were you like thinking the whole time,
"This is cafe"?
No, not really. I mean, I was a kid, so after a week or so,
I kind of was over it.
How long before cafe hit the table?
Probably, yeah, probably a week, I guess.
Oh, really?
I can't really remember.
It took a week to eat cafe?
No, my dad wouldn't kill it.
He would just take it to the butcher, yeah.
Your dad wouldn't kill cafe?
He would pay someone to do that?
Yeah, I mean--
I'm shocked.
Yeah, he can't--
You're not going to kill a cafe?
No, that's brutal, I think.
Most people can't do something like that.
Yeah, but then you got to pay some dude.
Your dad didn't have a lot of money.
Yeah, but it was a lot cheaper than buying the beef at the store.
That's why we did it, because the butcher didn't charge much,
and you could get like a year's worth of meat.
Off a calf.
Off a cow or a calf, yeah.
Really, a year's worth-- what do you do, you freeze it?
Uh-huh, we had a deep freeze, yeah.
But that's just-- that's a whole different thing
when you kill-- like killing a cow or like a pig,
that's something most farmers don't do.
They take it to the butcher to do it.
Wow.
That's really, really hard to do.
Real sad.
Geez.
Sorry to bum you out.
Yeah, man.
It's kind of funny to me.
But that's the whole story of Silence of the Land.
I mean, I don't-- I mean, you know, talk about great birthday gifts.
Shulie got me the best gift of anybody.
Which is?
He entered me in a bacon of the month club.
See, that's the problem with these movies,
like Jerry Seinfeld's Bee Movie,
even like trying to make bees nice little things.
Like now, everything becomes like human-like
when you take these animals and you start putting--
Well, after I saw--
Human attributes to them.
Like when Babe came out, that pig was so cute.
I never wanted to eat bacon again after that.
I don't eat bacon.
Where is he?
I don't eat it much.
I don't eat the pigs or the cows.
It's too highly evolved for me.
Chickens are probably too highly evolved.
I'm starting to get turned off to that.
Oh, come on, chicken taste good.
I love it.
I gave it all up.
It's fine.
But you never--
Well, you might go back.
No.
You know, it's funny because I had dinner
with my daughter last time.
My oldest daughter is like you, you know?
Yeah.
She's probably eating nothing.
Looking at that tofu.
And then she says, you know,
I'm thinking of going back to--
I don't eat tofu even.
That's too evolved for me.
And she actually doesn't like tofu either.
She says, but I'm--
She goes, I'm mostly into vegetables,
but I'm running out of things to eat.
I'm starting to think about some fish and chicken.
I go, I'm some father.
I go, why do you sell some chicken?
Come on.
You're going to do it in front of me.
You're going to do it.
Do it in front of me.
Yeah.
The guy I go back here is like,
Robin's like, you know,
she like quit smoking three weeks ago.
You know what I mean?
Nice.
All right.
She's trying.
I'm going to eat that stuff again, Robin.
Sure, sure, sure.
What about--
I've got restaurants with Robin
where we order some fish.
That woman, I've never seen her have sex,
but I've never seen a happier face
when she's eating that.
Right, right, right.
I like gourmet cooking.
So that's what we were eating.
It wasn't a fish with the bones in and the head on.
No.
So you cut out fish.
Did you cut out milk and cheese?
Everything.
Everything that comes from an animal.
Now listen to her.
She has a cold.
I don't have--
It's really working.
Have a roast beef sandwich.
My nose is stuffy.
Have a roast beef sandwich.
You're getting a cold.
I see what's going on.
Fine.
We'll see.
I'll be fine.
What is that voice today?
Kill a calf.
It's my nose being stuffed down.
Too many animals.
Too dry air.
You need some--
What?
All those colonics,
you're still getting a cold.
You need some caffeine part of the job.
Look, you're eating fish and chicken.
You get cold.
But you know, like, I go to the strip house,
and I'll get a big steak with the sauces and everything.
It's the greatest thing ever.
You never miss that.
No.
I never liked it as a kid.
It used to--
I used to gag on it.
My mother would force me to stand there
and eat the fucking steak.
I would just--
But you've had to have been to, like,
a gourmet steakhouse, right?
They had a filet mignon or something.
I don't even remember it.
At the layman, yeah.
Oh, man.
I don't even remember it.
A good steak,
you shouldn't have to put all the sauce on it.
Well, I know.
I'm kind of redneck, I guess.
I love sauce.
You've got a nice steak
at, like, one of these great steakhouse in New York.
You shouldn't put A1 all over it.
Now, rule--
No, I don't.
I don't.
Some of the--
Some of the one at Caffy's relative.
I know.
I'm thinking about poor little Caffy.
A descendant.
How old was Caffy when--
When you ate him.
When you ate him.
Well, he was still a calf,
but he was probably about--
I mean, I considered him a calf.
I guess he was probably about a year old, maybe.
Wow.
It's been so long.
How long did he weigh?
Probably maybe 800, 900 pounds, something like that.
When you would say, hey, Caffy, come here.
It's Richard.
Yeah.
Would he walk over to you?
Mm-hmm.
Oh, shit.
And then he would shit on your foot.
And then, like, would he ever, like, lay down on his back
or something and rub his belly?
No, he never was that affectionate.
But he would come over, and he would--
it was really cute.
He'd, like, nudge me with his head
because he knew I had a feed bucket and stuff.
And he mostly was friends with me
because I brought him feed, but--
Like, don't--
Yeah.
Did you like to scratch him and stuff?
Yeah.
Like, I had him on the head while he was eating,
and he wagged his tail.
And then I'd kiss him.
I never did any of that kind of stuff.
Whoa.
When I used to blow Caffy--
I didn't believe I was blowing my dinner.
Caffy, come over here.
I got my hip boots on.
Caffy, come here.
You're going to taste so good with ketchup or steak sauce.
I used to put Caffy's hind legs in the front of my boots,
and when I'd fuck him, he couldn't run away.
I got a feed bag and a blow job for you, Caffy.
He was stuff trained.
I'd tap him on the head.
He'd back up.
There was a guy in Kansas that--
Uh-oh.
Here we go.
They caught him in a barn hanging upside down,
and somehow he was banging a cow while he was hanging it.
Oh.
He had his feet tied to the top of the barn with a rope
and hanging upside down banging a cow.
Hey, Dad, what you doing?
It's called tantric cow sex.
Yeah.
Would you date it to crystal meth or something?
It must have been.
There's a lot of crystal meth going on out in, like, Kansas.
And you think it makes you screw cows?
Well, you know what it is.
No, I don't think it--
No, that's just a whole 'nother perversion.
But what it is is that we all have this image in our minds
of the small town and rural America.
